sentences.text: I am Paul Goldstein.
I was born in Antwerp, Belgium on December 27, 1932.
Our immediate family were my parents and my younger brother.
My mother's name was Nesie.
Maiden name Tiger, spelled like tiger.
She was a real tiger, too.
Goldstein.
My father was Joseph.
And my little brother, younger brother's name was Rene.
His birthday was August 14, 1936.
Yes.
My father's birthday was August 9, 1902, and my mother's was January 15, 1907.
Well, at the time they were born, it was this Austro-Hungarian Empire which was-- one day it was Poland, next day it-- [?
I say it ?] was Galicia.
Yes.
I believe in my father was born in a town called [?
Shamushul, ?] and my mother in a place called Kozlov, all in that same area.
I understand that when I was about a year and a half old, we took a train trip to show me to their parents, but I have no recollection of that whatsoever.
Well, they came in the early '20s, which is, I would say, about eight to nine years before I was born.
They were very poor families.
They both grew up without a father.
In one case, the father, I think, died young.
And the other one I think he just left home.
I think that the-- my-- my father's father, I believe, was-- was the one who passed away young.
So they were a family of seven children.
And they had to look after themselves.
And there were no great economic possibilities.
And there was early a trend because of the very strong anti-Semitism in the area for people to start migrating towards the west.
And Antwerp was one of the main destinations, Belgium for those people.
My father-- my mother-- well, he came first.
Then he brought my mother a couple of years later.
No, no.
They-- they married in-- in Belgium.
They married in Belgium.
That's what I think that-- I believe so.
All right.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- my parents were very hard-working people.
They started from scratch and opened up a beauty salon.
My father had quite a talent as a hairdresser.
And one shop after the other.
And eventually, ended up with one of the most prestigious salons in a beautiful area of the city near the National Bank, which was near the Central Park.
A very nice place with lots of staff, and the best people came to the beauty salon to do the hairstyling for many women.
Is there a name for the neighborhood?
I-- I know the-- I know the-- well, it-- it was called the better part of town.
It was near where the-- where the-- where the Palace of Justice was, the National Bank was, it was a better-to-do area of the city, if you look.
Yes, yes, he was pretty good.
In 1936, I believe he won a gold medal in Paris at the World's Fair.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, also very busy.
Both worked on their feet for seven days a week.
One of their big motivations was to help out their own parents in Poland.
Matter of fact, my father went back and bought them a house from the earnings that he-- that he had accumulated.
And so we were looked after by hired help, really.
And then Sunday we had a few hours together with our parents.
At the age of six-- so I'm talking now in 1938-- the-- I was enrolled in a-- in grade one in a special type of Jewish day school, which is little different from the systems we have in North America.
That kind of school would have two curriculums, or curricula, whichever way you say that.
And the-- and there would be two principals-- one for the official, regular, secular curriculum and then one for the Jewish curriculum with Hebrew teachers and principal.
That school was quite a famous school called the Tachkemoni school.
It-- it was reopened after the war.
It still exists and is going strong.
Yes, yes, yes, yes.
The choice was that in those days Belgium was still a bilingual country.
There are still two languages, but it's not bilingual anymore.
The northern part is unilingually Flemish, which is a Dutch dialect, and the southern part is unilingually Walloon which is a French dialect.
But in those days, it was totally bilingual.
You could choose the language of your education no matter where you lived.
So my dad said, well, we're going to alternate.
In grade one, you're going to go to the French-- Flemish curriculum.
Grade two, French, and then go back and forth like that.
So that's how I kept switching from the Dutch system to the French language system.
What was the effect on that for you?
The effect was-- was-- was-- there are many effects.

But what I remember the most was that since we had two ethnic groups that have always been at odds with each other, which would be a long story, goes back in history as far as the Roman occupation, believe it or not, the-- but both sides were very, very nationalistic.
And the-- the secular teachers, the Flemish or French teachers, were whipping up a lot of a nationalism among their own students.
So-- so it turned out that when we went at recess, we did not really play.
We divide in two camps, and we fought each other.
We were re-enacting the historic battles between the-- the Flemish peasantry and the French-- and the French occupants-- occupiers.
So this was quite something.
And really, it was a perfect example of how we can brainwash children.
So here we were, these little Jewish kids who had nothing to do with this-- with this particular history, and we were fighting out the-- the-- the Flemish-French battles in the courtyard.
And as a matter of fact, one of the kids had to be escorted home because the other ones would go after them.
It was really violent.
That was in grade one.
Well, the next grade, I'm in there from the other side.
All of a sudden, I was switching camps and fighting the other battle of the French against the Flemish.
This was quite-- quite traumatic how I kept changing sides, because, you know, my father didn't know this was going to happen.
So that's a-- a recollection.
The positive thing is that as a result I consider both Flemish, which is a Dutch dialect, and French my two mother tongues.
And I never lost them.
And I'm quite fluent in both.
This was a positive.
Well, a few.
I would say these are my two mother tongues.
Then when I managed to go to high school after the war, we had actually four languages of instruction.
There were three compulsory languages, which was the Dutch, French, and English.
And then we had German as an optional language, which was the fourth.
From home, I picked up Yiddish.
And then after the war, I-- when in Canada, when I went to university, I also took up some pretty advanced Spanish as well.
These are-- at one time, I knew these thoroughly, these languages.
And than a few others that I just have a smattering of.
A little bit of Polish because my parents were speaking Polish to each other, so we should understand what was going on, and I picked some up from that, yes.
All right.
The-- some of the interesting parts were that the-- the-- the difference between the two sets of instructions, the two cultures.
As a matter of fact, the Hebrew teachers were pretty-- teaching by rote-- very strict people.
And the other side was a little bit more lax.
Interesting that happened was that school actually stayed open until 1942.
And the Germans occupied Belgium, I believe, on May 10, 1940.
That's when they invaded.
And as a result, they-- once they got organized, the school was still running, but they dismissed the Hebrew people, and they replaced them with a-- a pro-- a pro-German principal.
And some of the Flemish Gentile teachers were, in a way, collaborators with the occupying troops, forces, and started to appear in brown shirts, kind of SS uniforms.
And they were very, very rough on the Jewish children.

This was part of the-- that lasted only till the summer of '42 because summer of '42, that's when the Germans, after a lot of-- imposed a lot of restrictions on the community, isolating them, and identifying them, and with some-- and-- and with a lot of the things which we'll come to, perhaps, later.
They finally then decided this-- this was the time to start deporting and-- and-- and destroying the community.
And that's when the school closed.
There still was, but the-- but the--
There were still Jewish teachers teaching it.
But the emphasis, the authority, was more or less in the hands of pro-- of pro-German staff, which obviously were not-- there were no longer a Hebrew principal.

And, as a matter of fact, our friendly Gentile principal had been replaced as well, all staffed by pro-- and the teachers, the-- the ones that do the Brownshirts, they-- they-- they delighted in beating up the kids once in a while in class for no reason and insulting us for being Jewish and all that because they were actually Nazis.
Yes.
Totally non-Jewish neighborhood.
We were not observant, although I was aware that my father was very Jewish in a nationalistic way, in an ethnic identifying way, very proud.
But he was not raised in the tradition himself because he didn't have a father when he was young.
Had to go to work.
So it was totally non-observant.
But, as I said, ethnically, very much identifying with the Jewish community.
Very proud, as well, of our heritage.

So as a result, we-- the language we spoke at home was actually the local Flemish, which was the Dutch dialect, and-- whereas most of the Jewish community would not speak that language because it was considered the language of the peasantry or the poor people.
And it was looked down at, and the better-to-do people would all speak French.
French was the language of the aristocracy.
Flemish was the language of the other-- of the proletariat, if you want, and of the local people.
And French was a world language, and Flemish was only a local language.
So-- also, most of the more religious Jews would naturally speak Yiddish.
Since we were-- since we were not observant, and-- and we lived in this particular neighborhood.
And my father, as a matter of fact, didn't even speak French because he was not-- he-- he had never really had the occasion to be able to go to grade school, I believe, because of his background.
As a result, we spoke Flemish.

And we spoke Flemish like native-- native people, so our-- you could not tell by our-- our accent that were not-- that we were Jewish, which, in a way, was one of the major reasons why we survived the war, which is paradoxical.
But this is-- this is--
No.
No, only in school.

But then since our neighborhood-- there were-- I don't recall having any Jewish children in our neighborhood, so most of the kids I played with were neighbors' kids, which were all Christian, mostly Catholic.
That was the main religion there.
Only because we had an aunt and an uncle, my late father's brother.
Plus we also had--
Jacques.
And-- and we also had a sister of my mother's.
Her name was Rosa.
So I had an aunt and an uncle and their families.
So I recall that only on the major holidays we-- we-- we met to celebrate those like we did.
It's Passover, Rosh Hashanah, but that was about the--
Mostly, it was in our home because there were-- we were a little bit better off than they were.
They didn't make out as well as my-- as my parents did.
Things began to change when, first of all there were the rumblings about what was going on in-- in Poland after the occupation of Poland, I think in September, 1939.
And then there were rumblings about Hitler, the Jewish-- his attitude to the Jewish people.
But things pretty well remained peaceful from a child's perspective.
A lot of the things I'm telling you is-- is things I learned later on.
But-- but putting myself from my vantage point, I would say things were pretty well stable.
All-- all-- all that changed was a heightened nervous tension at home because of my parents' anxieties about their families in Poland.
And-- and then they're starting to speak their own native language, which so we shouldn't understand.
That was the main change at home.
Now the big change occurred on May 10 when all of a sudden, shooting started, bombs were flying, planes would came over, and before we saw-- we knew it, the Germans rolled into the-- into the area.
And it was always a prized possession for all-- all armies throughout history because being such an important seaport.
So because of the rumors, whoever could afford transportation fled.
So the big change was that we all-- we all packed up, rented a little truck, put our possessions on it, and tried to escape in front of the-- the troops.
Then we got into France, and everybody was on these roads south.
And mainly towards the-- there was a French coast to be able to get over to England.
But then we ran into the retreating Allied troops-- British troops-- on the way to Dunkirk.
And roads-- roads simply got so clogged that it wasn't even funny.
So I remember how we-- we-- we ate from buying eggs and-- from farms on the way.
And then one day, the Germans caught up with us.
And I recall the first German I ever saw, officer, was a-- a military policeman on a bicycle.
I think they call them the-- the-- the Feldgendarmerie.
And they had special uniforms.
This first guy, I'll never forget.
As a matter of fact, once, I wrote an essay in-- in college in Canada when I came over here.

I always-- I-- the title, I wrote a story that was called, 'The German Army in Victory and Defeat.' And in victory, he was my symbol because he was absolutely a strapping man, very heavy set, clean shaven, uniform, still see it, like brand new.
Boots, everything shiny.
His motorcycle just came out of a factory.
And then he-- and then they-- they kind of-- As a matter of fact, before-- just before they caught up with us, they were also bombing the roads with-- with-- with fire bombs.
And then we had to-- we had to dive into the fields and into the ditches because we were all mixed up with the Allied troops, you see.
So we were-- the first time I was really fired upon in my life.
So after they caught up with us, we couldn't just continue.
Then we had to make an about-turn, and  we all returned to where we came from, except for a handful of people who be able to trickle out of the ranks and disappear.
So that was the first big change-- an exodus and a return to a totally different lifestyle.
This was May--
I was-- looking for ages-- I was nine.
My brother was five.
From there on, things started to change because the Germans had a program where they gradually started to intimidate, isolate, and-- and organize the Jewish community for deportation.

I remember-- I remember that first of all, we all had to wear the yellow Star of David with the words-- I think, three languages, or at least two-- the word 'Jew' in Dutch, which was 'Jood,' J-O-O-D, and then 'Juif' for the French part, J-U-I-F.
This you had to wear.
We were told that not wearing this and getting caught, there would be a severe penalty.
So this is the first thing.
Sewing on the yellow star.
Obviously, we're all walking around with stars.
The next thing I know was that the-- all Jewish enterprises had to have a sign on-- on their window saying in three languages-- German, Dutch, and French-- Jewish Enterprise.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] These were the three languages.
So they would be identified.
So-- so part of the propaganda was to boycott Jewish enterprises.
So therefore, all of a sudden, our business was also targeted because of that sign.
Next thing.
Next thing, we were-- the main mode of transportation was streetcars.
And most street-- and some streetcars had a-- just one car, and then some had a second car attached.
So then, the next thing was the Jews were restricted with the stars only on the first platform of the second car.
So, a lot of cars have no second car.
We just couldn't use public transportation anymore.
It was a kind of segregation of transportation.
After that, curfew.
Jews were not allowed outside of their homes after a certain time.
It was either 7 o'clock or 8 o'clock.
And all these things gradually changed everything.
You see.
Well, first of all, we're still going to the same school.
We-- we didn't go outside much.
So in school, there was no problem, because we're all Jewish kids in that particular-- in Tachkemoni.
And now, what also happened was that some of the neighborhood kids were-- would stop playing with us because their parent told us not to, their parents.
This I remember.
And some still would.
My parents, my father was always a defiant person, not prone to cooperate, so after a little while, we stopped wearing the stars.
We just took our chances, so we wouldn't be subject to whatever would befall people who did wear the stars.
So we stopped wearing them.
Parents as well, yes.
Which was against the rules and the law, but that's what we did.
Well, at one particular time, my father found someone [? who also ?] changed our name.
So that-- and we got some-- for-- for-- not fundamentally, but just for general public and also with some papers just in case we were checked on the street.

Because, the-- as a matter of fact, in '42, in the-- in the spring of '42, they-- they required all people in the Jewish faith or ethnically Jewish should report, register, with the Judenrat, the Jewish councils, and they would then be issued special identity papers.
Whether they were Belgians or not, they would have a big-- they would be considered-- even if they were born elsewhere, even if they'd been there 20 years or 30 years-- they'd be called strangers.
On their card would be a big stamp that would say 'Jew' in two languages.
There would be a star of David.
There would also be the number of the-- of the registration number with the Jewish community.
This was all, we didn't realize, all planning for, to be more efficient later on when they were starting to deport the people and arrest them.
Yes, yes.
I was able to, somehow, find it and keep it.
And-- and-- and--
I believe it was Coltex.
I don't know the reason.
C-O-L-T-E-X.
For a little while, we went under that name, just when the things got rougher towards the end of that two-year transitional period.
Well, there were no direct persecutions.
That phase-- during that phase, there were no deportations yet.
So-- so-- as I-- after the war, I studied this whole phenomenon of anti-Semitic persecution quite-- quite in depth.
It became one of my main subjects of interest.
I spent a lot of time on that.

And I then studied, and I discovered that throughout the whole Christian era, whenever Jews are persecuted and put in ghettos and isolated, that it was always a Jewish reaction to apply towards persecution what was called the policy of accommodation.
This was a traditional reaction by Jewish communities through their leadership.
This worked because it had never really been the official policy of the church or any government to completely destroy or annihilate the Jews.
They were supposed to be persecuted, yes, to be deprived of certain occupations, herded into certain areas, extra tax imposed on them.
But it was never the policy.
So when this went on, the general Jewish reaction being so outnumbered was-- was to give in, kind of cooperate.
So, that-- it would bend with the wind because it will blow over eventually, which it always did.
So there was no-- and the leadership, that was the policy of the leadership.
Just do what is being asked, and you'll be OK.
That was the general reaction.
Was that your father's point of view?
It wasn't his-- it wasn't his instinctive point of view, because he was always a rebel and very, very proud and tough and very gutsy person.
But there weren't too many like him.
He didn't have too much company.

And as long as we lived where we lived, and he made a decent living, and because he had established a marvelous relationship with most of our clientele, so they didn't abandon us, we-- we didn't know what was going to happen eventually.

Everybody thought and hoped and felt that as long as we would cooperate, this was only a matter of time, and eventually, you know, like historically, this was only a period in our lives, and nothing really major would happen.
That was the hope.
We knew better afterwards.
We didn't know at the time.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Well-- well, first of all, I was very young.
And my father was a very, I would say, quite authoritarian person.
So we weren't too much exposed to-- to news events.
As a matter of fact, they try not to have us hear what is going on, so we shouldn't be disturbed.

What happened next was that we reached a point in time which I now realize was the-- was a result of the decision by the Nazi government to embark on the complete, on the so-called, quote, 'Final Solution.' So the effect of it was that there was a system to it.
The German efficiency was also applied to-- the traditional German efficiency was applied to this exercise as well.
So the first step was to have all-- all heads of families, able-bodied men, report for forced labor.
They were told that they should report, very short notice.
And everything would be OK.
They would simply be taken for a while to help the Germans with their war effort.
And it was an organization called Organisation Todt, T-O-D-T, under Albert Speer, I believe, which really was always in charge of slave labor.
And the first-- the people who were rounded up in our town were shipped off to the French coast to help build the so-called Atlantic wall, series of fortification against a possible Allied invasion.
Yes.
This included my father.
Yes, his brother as well.
Jacques as well.
So they-- as a matter of fact, I recall that my father, through a lawyer friend and some money, managed to buy himself into a-- a one-month extension, which we celebrated quite fervently.
And after that month, he had no more extension.
And then one day, my father's just-- the-- the local police came, escorted my father away, and that was it.
Yes.
So I was left then with-- with my mother and-- and my little brother in that home where we lived.
What did my father do?
Well, they didn't tell him they were going to shoot you or kill you.
They said, if you come peacefully, you'll be OK.
And it's just for a while.
And your family will be left undisturbed.
If you resist, it will be much direr consequences.
So under the circumstances, hoping for the best, people had no choice.
There was no alternative.
There was not-- not an option.
